返回本批次

互操作性可以拯救开放网络 (2023)

Interoperability Can Save the Open Web (2023)

spectrum.ieee.org 作者 janandonly 分数 167 评论 52 2026/03/26 22:51
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 作者科里·多克托罗 (Cory Doctorow) 在他的新书《互联网骗局：如何抓住计算手段》中提出了颠覆大型科技的有力案例。虽然 Twitter、Facebook、Instagram 或亚马逊等互联网平台的主导地位常常被认为是理所当然的，但多克托罗认为，这些围墙花园是由法律结构围起来的，而不是工程壮举。多克托罗提出强制互操作性（任何给定平台与另一个平台交互的能力）作为打破这些障碍并使互联网更加自由和民主的一种方式。IEEE Spectrum 撰稿人迈克尔·诺兰与多克托罗讨论了他的新书以及互操作性如何打破技术领域和其他领域的垄断。正如您在致谢中所写的那样，您的新书《互联网大会》“体现了二十年来对数字人权问题的写作和研究的倡导价值。”这是如何以互操作性作为打破大型科技垄断的一种方式的争论的形式？科里·多克托罗乔纳森·沃思科里·多克托罗：在我参与技术的几十年里，用户一方的实体确实发生了变化。有时是科技平台或公司，有时不是。有时是政府，有时不是。在开始捍卫那些确实得到用户支持的娱乐公司的科技公司后，我意识到它们之间的区别并不在于一个行业由娱乐高管组成，他们对人权的承诺非常薄弱，另一个行业由科技高管组成，他们对人权的承诺非常薄弱。当一个部门极度集中时，那些愿意用公共利益和基本民主价值观来换取雇主盈利能力增量增长的人就会在公司内部获得听证会并接管公司的决策。当企业不必担心因滥用客户而失去客户时，人们会争论“我们不应该这样做，因为这是错误的，而且对企业不利”只能争论“我们不应该这样做，因为这是错误的”。他们不得不勉强承认这可能对生意有利。该州的任何公司最终都会对人权构成严重危害。正如布兰迪斯所说，我们应该真正适应这种“大的诅咒”，而且这种诅咒确实有害。这引出了本书的主要建议，即重申系统和平台之间的互操作性可以使这些非常大的公司分崩离析。你能定义互操作性吗？Doctorow：从根本上讲，它只是一种东西与另一种东西一起使用的能力。将打印机中的任何墨水与任何纸张一起使用，将任何袜子与鞋子一起使用，将任何人的汽油放入您的汽车中，将任何灯泡放入您的电灯插座中。有自愿的、强制性的互操作性，一群利益相关者聚集在一起，他们说：“这是我们希望所有产品都能实现的目标，我们将设计一个框架，以便确保每个灯泡在插入电灯插座时都会亮起。”还有一些他们漠不关心的事情：汽车公司不会阻止你在你的车上安装一个点烟器转 USB 适配器。公司可以快速发展，因为技术已经获得了这些巨大的网络效应，但由于互操作性，它们的转换成本也非常低。——科里·多克托罗然后是第三种互操作性，一种有嚼劲的、有趣的、有丰富互联网历史的互操作性，这就是对抗性互操作性，在书中我们称之为“comcom”，是竞争性兼容性的缩写。这是违背原始设备制造商意愿的互操作：抓取、逆向工程、机器人，所有这些在面对积极敌意时所做的粗糙的事情。这就像 Apple 对 Microsoft Office 进行逆向工程并制作 iWork 套件（Pages、Numbers 和 Keynote）一样，这样任何拥有 Mac 的人都可以阅读任何基于 Windows 的 Office 文件，而无需从 Microsoft 购买任何软件。技术史上有很多这样的例子。它确实是技术的引擎。这就是我们在科技公司内部经历令人难以置信的繁荣与萧条周期的原因。公司可以快速发展，因为技术具有巨大的网络效应，但由于互操作性，它们的转换成本也非常低。任何时候一个平台不适合你，或者有人有更好的想法，他们都可以制作一个工具，让你离开上一个平台并跳转到新平台。由于这种互操作性，真正发展壮大的公司成为其他想要进入的公司不可抗拒的目标，吸引用户离开，并为他们提供一条从旧地方到新地方的非常简单的途径。我认为互操作性被中断解释了“为什么科技公司曾经表现不错，但现在却表现不佳？”这是因为用户无法离开。如果你不能离开，他们也不必善待你。那么，技术互操作性是如何中断的呢？Doctorow：有很多平台实际上已经成为围墙花园。争论的焦点是，这些企业之所以如此坚固，以至于没有人扰乱它们或取代它们，是因为它们拥有如此优秀的工程师。我总是从人们那里听到有关 iOS 的说法，他们说：“iOS 是一个坚不可摧的金库，他们已经找到了如何阻止人们这样做的方法。”我说：“等一下——尝试这种行为是重罪。”如果我说我是世界冠军拳击手，没有人打败过我，但我也可以因为你试图夺取我的头衔而让你入狱五年，我们怎么知道我是一名多么优秀的拳击手？为什么他们聘请律师而不是工程师，为什么他们首选律师而不是工程师，对我来说，答案是因为律师能够更有效地阻止有资本、有动力的竞争对手想出如何绕过他们的技术对策。相反，这是法律规定的转换成本。如何修改这些法律以使现有平台更具互操作性？Doctorow：我感兴趣的事情之一是如何制定一项无法欺骗的法律。 Facebook 经常提出只有他们才能遵循的规则。互操作性规则有两个限制：它们必须是可管理的并且不能构成资本障碍。例如，我们可以为 Twitter [现在 X] 提出两种不同的规则。其中一个是端到端原则，该规则规定：如果我关注某人，并且他们发布了某些内容，我就可以看到它。这条规则使得 Twitter 很难过多地关注其首选供应商的内容。最重要的是，我们添加了退出权。这是在不失去关注者和被关注者的情况下离开 Twitter 的权利。这将是建立 API 的授权，并且可能是 ActivityPub API。这是一个非常好的标准，它的缺陷比我们设计新标准更容易弥补。你把这两者放在一起，Twitter 不能囚禁任何人，Twitter 必须善待那些选择留下来的人。你还认为，要求互操作性将导致某种良性的反垄断循环。打破技术垄断是否会削弱他们强化从一开始就阻止互操作性的政策的能力？多克托罗：这是一半。要了解一个行业成功实现其监管目标，您必须了解它需要两件事。首先是超额利润形式的权力，它可以用来游说。第二是团结。回想一下 Napster 战争。这是一个由数百家中小型科技公司组成的行业，这些公司的利润加起来比整个音乐行业和整个电影行业还要多。但电影业和音乐业的参与人数较少：以前音乐是五人，电影是七人，现在是三人、四人。结果，他们就如何花钱达成了一致。科技产业无处不在。他们在背后互相捅刀子。科技行业对待人们就像 20 年前音乐行业对待人们一样，原因就在于团结。竞争的两个好处是，它打破了用于制定公共政策的现金储备，并引入了集体行动问题，使剩余储备更难花掉。然后，这种良性循环如何从科技延伸到其他行业？ 多克托罗：想想 20 世纪上半叶标准石油公司解体时发生了什么。标准石油公司并不是唯一的信托公司。一切都存在信托：威士忌、铁路、钢铁、铝、汽车。标准石油公司的主导地位让人们对是否能够拥有一个负责任的政府感到如此绝望，以至于标准石油公司的垮台打开了政治意愿的闸门，导致所有其他信任都破灭了。我想追随科技公司，因为它具有这种特有的互操作性，这使其成为软目标。我们从技术开始，这给了我们动力、信誉和政治意愿来追随其他人。科里·多克托罗是一位作家、活动家、记者和博主。他的新书《The Internet Con: How to Seize the Means of Computation》由 Verso Books 出版，于 2023 年 9 月 5 日上市。他是电子前沿基金会的特别顾问。

Original Summary

  1. In his new book The Internet Con: How to Seize the Means of Computation, author Cory Doctorow presents a strong case for disrupting Big Tech. While the dominance of Internet platforms like Twitter, Facebook, Instagram, or Amazon is often taken for granted, Doctorow argues that these walled gardens are fenced in by legal structures, not feats of engineering. Doctorow proposes forcing interoperability—any given platform’s ability to interact with another—as a way to break down those walls and to make the Internet freer and more democratic.IEEE Spectrum contributor Michael Nolan spoke with Doctorow about his new book and how interoperability could break up monopolies both in tech and beyond.Your new book, The Internet Con, as you write in its acknowledgements, “crystallizes two decades’ worth of advocacy writing about and working on issues in digital human rights.” How did that come to take the form of an argument for interoperability as a way to break up Big Tech monopolies?Cory DoctorowJonathan WorthCory Doctorow: Over the decades that I’ve been involved in technology, the entities that are on the user’s side have really changed. Sometimes it was tech platforms or companies and sometimes it wasn’t. Sometimes it was governments and sometimes it wasn’t. Having started off defending tech companies that really did have their users’ backs from entertainment companies, I realized that the distinction between them was not that one industry was made up of entertainment executives whose commitment to human rights was very thin and the other was made up of tech executives who had a more good-faith commitment. When a sector is extremely concentrated, the people who are willing to trade the public good and foundational democratic values for incremental increases in their employer’s profitability get a hearing within the company and take over the company’s decision making. When a business doesn’t have to worry about losing its customers due to abusing them, then the people arguing, “We shouldn’t do this because it’s wrong and also it’s bad for business” can only argue, “We shouldn’t do this because it’s wrong.” They have to grudgingly admit that it might be good for business. Any firm in that state eventually becomes a serious hazard to human rights. It’s this “curse of bigness,” as Brandeis called it, that we should really be attuned to and is really pernicious.That leads to the main proposal of the book, which is that reaffirming interoperability between systems and platforms can break apart these very large companies. Can you define interoperability?Doctorow: At its root, it’s just the ability to use one thing with something else. Use any ink in your printer with any paper, use any socks with your shoes, anyone’s gasoline in your car, put any lightbulb in your light socket. There’s voluntary, mandatory interoperability, where a group of stakeholders get together and they say, “This is the goal we want all of our products to achieve, and we are going to design a framework so that we can make sure that every lightbulb lights up when you stick it in a light socket.” Then there’s the stuff where they’re indifferent: Car companies don’t stop you from putting a little cigarette-lighter-to-USB adapter into your car. Companies can grow very quickly because tech has got these great network effects, but they also have, because of interoperability, really low switching costs.—Cory DoctorowThen there’s the third kind of interop, the kind of chewy, interesting, lots-of-rich-Internet-history interop, which is adversarial interoperability, which in the book we call “comcom,” short for competitive compatibility. It’s the interop that’s done against the wishes of the original equipment manufacturer: scraping, reverse engineering, bots, all of that gnarly stuff done in the face of active hostility. This would be like Apple reverse-engineering Microsoft Office and making the iWork suite—Pages, Numbers, and Keynote—so that anyone with a Mac could read any Windows-based office file without having to buy any software from Microsoft. There are so many examples of this from technology’s history. It’s really the engine of technology. It’s the reason we’ve had this incredible boom-and-bust cycle within tech companies. Companies can grow very quickly because tech has got these great network effects, but they also have, because of interoperability, really low switching costs. Anytime a platform isn’t suited to you or someone has a better idea for it, they can just make a tool [that] kind of greases the skids for you to leave the last one and to jump to the new one. Because of that interoperability, the companies that grow really big become irresistible targets for other companies that want to come in, tempt their users away, and offer them a very easy path to get from the old place to the new one. I think interoperability being interrupted explains “Why are tech companies bad when they used to be good?” It’s because the users can’t leave. If you can’t leave, they don’t have to treat you well.So how has tech interoperability become interrupted?Doctorow: There are lots of platforms that have become, effectively, walled gardens. The argument is that the reason that these businesses are so sturdy, that no one has disrupted them or displaced them is that they have such good engineers. I get this from people about iOS all the time, saying “iOS is an impregnable vault, they’ve figured out how to stop people from doing it.” and I say, “wait a second—it’s a felony to try.” If I say I’m the world champion boxer, and no one has ever defeated me, but I can also send you to prison for five years for trying to take my title, how do we know how good a boxer I am? The question of why they use the lawyers and not the engineers, why their go-to is the lawyers and not the engineers, to me the answer is because the lawyers are much more effective at preventing a capitalized, motivated rival from figuring out how to bypass their technical countermeasures.So you’re saying it’s not technical dominance or business superiority or network effects that keep these platforms dominant; it’s instead the switching costs imposed by law. How could those laws be changed to make existing platforms more interoperable?Doctorow: One of the things I’m interested in is how to make a law you can’t cheat on. Facebook often proposes rules only they can follow. Rules for interoperability have two constraints: they have to be administrable and cannot constitute a capital barrier. For example, we can propose two different rules for Twitter [now X]. One would be an end-to-end principle, which is a rule that says: If I follow someone, and they post something, I can see it. That rule makes it really hard for Twitter to overweight content from its preferred suppliers. On top of that we add the Right to Exit. This is the right to leave Twitter without losing your followers and followees. This would be a mandate to stand up an API, and it would be, probably, an ActivityPub API. It’s a pretty good standard and its deficiencies can be remedied more easily than we can design a new standard. You put those two together, Twitter can’t keep anyone prisoner, and Twitter will have to treat the people who choose to stay well.You also argue that requiring interoperability will lead to something of a virtuous antitrust cycle. Is it that breaking down tech monopolies lessens their ability to reinforce the policies that have prevented interoperability in the first place?Doctorow: That’s half of it. To understand the success of an industry in achieving its regulatory goals, you have to understand it needs two things. The first thing is power in the form of excess profits it can maneuver to lobbying. The second is unity.Think back to the Napster wars. That was a sector of hundreds of small and medium-sized tech companies that in aggregate had more profit than the whole music industry and the whole movie industry. But the movie industry and the music industry had a smaller number of participants: music was five, movies were seven back then, now it’s three and four. As a result, they were able to agree on what to spend the money on. The tech industry was all over the place. They were knifing each other in the back. The reason the tech industry treats people like the music industry treated them 20 years ago is that unity.The two benefits of competition are that it breaks the cash reserves that are used to enact public policy and it introduces the collective action problem that makes the remaining reserves harder to spend.How does that virtuous cycle then extend from tech into other sectors?Doctorow: Think about what happened with the breakup of Standard Oil in the first part of the 20th century. Standard Oil was not the only trust. There were trusts for everything: whiskey, railroads, iron, aluminum, cars. Standard Oil’s dominance made people so hopeless about whether or not they could have an accountable government that the toppling of Standard Oil opened up a floodgate of political will that saw all of those other trusts shattered.I want to go after tech because it has this characteristic interoperability that makes it a soft target. We start with tech, and that gives us the momentum, the credibility, and the political will to go after everybody else.Cory Doctorow is an author, activist, journalist, and blogger. His new book, The Internet Con: How to Seize the Means of Computation, from Verso Books, is available 5 September 2023. He is special advisor to the Electronic Frontier Foundation.

评论（默认中文）

L_Rahman 2026/03/27 00:14
我们真正幸运的一件事是 Claude Code 而不是 ChatGPT 应用程序赢得了定义 AI 产品的战争，并且它在您的文件系统上运行。但现实却不同，一切都必须经过封闭应用程序层上的 API，而我们都恳求 OpenAI 将 XYZ 端点添加到他们的平台。

Anthropic 现在正在竞相缩小这一差距，因为他们意识到没有锁定。如果产品只是具有层次结构的 .md 文件，您可以将任何工具和智能放在其上。默认情况下它是可互操作的，甚至可能不是故意的。

我们应该尽一切可能阻止他们在未来 18 个月内尝试的大锁定。

One of the things we got really lucky with is that Claude Code and not the ChatGPT app won the war for the defining AI product and it runs on your filesystem. There's a different reality where everything had to go through the API on a closed app layer and we're all begging OpenAI to add XYZ endpoint to their platform.

Anthropic is now racing to close this gap because they realize there's no lock-in. If the product is just .md files with hierarchy, you can drop any harness and intelligence on top of it. It is interoperable by default, possibly not even by intention.

We should do everything possible to stop the great lock-in that they'll attempt in the next 18 months.

在 HN 中查看
zoogeny 2026/03/27 05:58
我认为这不会持续下去，因为文件树中的结构化文本文件相对于 API 提供的任何优势都将通过某种比文件系统更好的格式来最大化。

这可能意味着倾向于 SQLite 或其他一些开放格式。我自己的想法是，文档的图形结构最终将比树形结构或数据库结构更有价值。

但是，如果文件系统中任何篡夺结构化文本的专有实现变得流行，那么该公司将拥有巨大的影响力。

I don't think this can last, because whatever advantage structured text files in a file tree provides over an API will be maximized by some format that is better than a file system.

That might mean leaning into SQLite or some other open format. My own thought is that a graph-like structure of documents will be ultimately more valuable than either a tree-like structure or a database-like structure.

But if a proprietary implementation of whatever usurps structured text in a file-system becomes popular, that company will have significant leverage.

在 HN 中查看
good-idea 2026/03/27 07:31
我认为文件夹中的文件有两个优点，即法学硕士和人类能够浏览和轻松阅读。

但我也同意这有局限性。如果我向你提出挑战，并说黑曜石解决了这个问题，或者接近解决这个问题，你会说还有哪些差距尚未实现？

我是因为我正在这个领域做一些事情，而你的评论涉及到我的三个主要成分：文本文件、SQLite 和图表。

Two advantages I see to files in folders are the ability to browse and the ability to easily read - by both LLMs and humans.

But I also agree that this has limitations. If I were to challenge you and say that Obsidian solves this, or gets close to solving it, what gaps would you say were left unfulfilled?

I am because I'm working on something in this space and your comment touches on my three main ingredients: text files, SQLite, and graphs.

在 HN 中查看
mentalgear 2026/03/27 04:18
这解释得最好：

> 当 Facebook 诞生时，它为已经在使用 MySpace 的人们提供了一个工具，可以冒充您登录 MySpace，收集您的朋友给您留下的所有消息，并将它们放入您的 Facebook 收件箱中。您可以在那里回复它们，它会将它们发送回您的 MySpace 发件箱，以便您的朋友可以看到它们。这就是 Facebook 能够如此迅速地从 MySpace 夺走如此多用户的原因。这就是互操作性的意义所在。但如果你今天试图这样做，Facebook 就会利用那些以不同方式执行的法律，或者在 MySpace 时代根本不存在的法律来毁掉你。

> 如果我们要恢复这种技术互操作性的“高贵的古代艺术”，那些对他们使用的平台明显不满的用户会认为成本足够低，可以离开并加入更好的空间。反过来，这些公司规模会更小，会更加关注用户满意度，并且无法绕过试图追究其责任的政府。

https://www.greeneuropeanjournal.eu/cory-doctorow-how-to-tak...

This explains it best:

> When Facebook was born, it gave people who were already using MySpace a tool that would pretend to be you and log into MySpace, collect all the messages that your friends had left for you, and put them in your Facebook inbox. You could reply to them there, and it would send them back to your MySpace outbox, so your friends would see them. And that was what allowed Facebook to take so many users from MySpace so quickly. This is what interoperability is about. But if you tried to do that today, Facebook would use laws that were either enforced differently or did not even exist at the time of MySpace, to ruin you.

> If we were to restore this “noble ancient art” of technological interoperability, the users who are so obviously discontent with the platforms they use would consider the costs low enough to leave and join better spaces. In turn, the companies would be smaller, would pay more attention to user satisfaction, and could not push around the governments that tried to hold them to account.

https://www.greeneuropeanjournal.eu/cory-doctorow-how-to-tak...

在 HN 中查看
julianlam 2026/03/27 11:53
多克托罗最近在蒙特利尔的 FediMTL 上（远程）发表了讲话。他像往常一样列出了赌注，但补充说，在加拿大，法律不允许我们对竞争对手的 API 进行逆向工程，作为某些自由贸易谈判的一部分，我们放弃了这一权利。
Doctorow recently spoke (remotely) at FediMTL in Montreal. He laid out the stakes as usual, but added that in Canada, we are legally not allowed to reverse engineer competitor APIs, and we gave up this right as part of some free trade negotiation.
在 HN 中查看
bitwize 2026/03/27 06:26
但、但是……真是独角兽般的成长……！
B-but... muh unicorn-like growth...!
在 HN 中查看
renegat0x0 2026/03/26 23:34
我什么都不是。我影响不大。我希望我的程序能够免受政府入侵、年龄检查、加密后门和公司监视。如何赢得这场与科技巨头的战斗？

我深深地自我寄宿。自托管要想取得成功，就必须变得更好、不受监管且免费。它需要易于分发。数据应该易于分发。导入和导出应该快速且简单。

这就是为什么我的大多数程序都使用人类可读的 JSON，或者使用复制粘贴的 SQLite 表。

我是从波兰来的。我的祖先通过躲藏和小规模的党派斗争得以生存。我对软件的看法是“党派”。它以小型、分布式的方式与大型科技公司作战。

我不确定，但我认为我所说的与互操作性类似。

I am nobody. I have little impact. I want my programs to be safe from government intrusions, from age checks, from encryption backdoors, from corporate surveillance. How do I win this battle with big tech?

I am deeply in self-host. For the self-host to succeed it needs to be better, unregulated, and free. It needs to be easily distributed. The data should be easily distributed. Import and export should be fast and easy.

That is why most of my programs use JSONs that are human readable, or use SQLite tables that are just copy-paste away.

I am from Poland. My ancestors were able to survive by hiding, and by fighting small partisan battles. My idea of software is "partisan". It battles big tech in small, distributed ways.

I am not sure, but I think what I said is similar to interoperability.

在 HN 中查看
embedding-shape 2026/03/26 23:50
> 我什么都不是。我影响不大。我希望我的程序能够免受政府入侵、年龄检查、加密后门和公司监视。如何赢得这场与科技巨头的战斗？

如果你只是具体谈论 你的 没有人可以访问的程序，我认为没有任何战斗？做你想做的事，没有人关心，甚至不会知道。

如果您谈论的是向其他人提供免费和开源的软件，我也不认为这里有任何战斗可以赢得。

当人们谈论网络不开放，或“年龄检查”和“后门”等时，他们主要谈论的是营利性平台，这些平台让用户“使用”他们的平台来换取某些东西。这些可能不应该是“为所欲为，后果自负”，而是对它们进行某种检查，所以我们最终不会让商界人士急于建立折磨关系。

即使平台必须有年龄检查、加密后门和一大堆其他“坏东西”或只是“烦人的东西”，我也不认为自托管生态系统有太多需要担心的，我们都已经“没有保证”地运行软件，而且我在家运行的很多东西都是我自己编写的，当然我不会关心年龄检查或其他什么，即使它被监管为强制的。

> I am nobody. I have little impact. I want my programs to be safe from government intrusions, from age checks, from encryption backdoors, from corporate surveillance. How do I win this battle with big tech?

If you're only talking specifically about your program that no one else has access to, I don't think there is any battle? Do whatever you want, no one cares nor would even know about it.

If you're talking about making software available for others, for free and open source, I also don't think there is any battles to be won here.

When people talk about the web not being open, or "age checks" and "backdoors" and so on, they're mainly talking about for-profit platforms, that let users "use" their platform in exchange for something. These probably shouldn't be "do whatever you want, consequences be damned" but instead have some sort of checks against them, so we don't end up letting the business-people rush towards building torment nexuses.

Even if platforms has to have age checks, encryption backdoors and a whole slew of other "bad stuff" or just "annoying stuff", I don't think the self-hosted ecosystem has much to worry about, we all run software "without warranties" already, and plenty of the stuff I'm running at home I've written myself, of course I won't care about age checks or whatever, even if it was regulated to be forced.

在 HN 中查看
freedomben 2026/03/27 00:06
在一个大型科技公司和政府要求面向用户的事物做事（例如年龄验证等）并对他们的行为负责的世界中 用户 这样做，即使是自托管也会成为问题，除非您是唯一的用户。 有很多人仍在这样做，但他们可能正在承担自己没有意识到的责任。 例如，如果我建立了一个自托管的 git forge 并允许其他人使用它，而一些我不认识的用户将 CSAM 提交到他们的存储库，引用（解释为我不记得确切）《巫师》中的 Dijkstra：这就是所谓的陷入困境，而你也陷入了困境。
In a world where big tech and governments are requiring user-facing things to do things (like age verification, etc) and be liable for what their users do with it, even the self-host becomes a problem unless you are your only user. There are plenty of people that are still doing it, but they're probably taking on liability they don't realize. For example if I stand up a self-hosted git forge and allow others to use it, and some user I don't know commits CSAM to their repo, to quote (paraphrased cause I don't remember exactly) Dijkstra from The Witcher: That's called being in the shit, and you're in the shit.
在 HN 中查看
taeric 2026/03/27 00:25
我的意思是，很多事情都是如此？ 一直都是这样。

如果您经常在家里招待朋友吃饭，没有人会说您必须遵守与餐厅相同的规则。 你开始让其他人在你家举办晚宴，事情可能会发生变化。 你开始让人们向你征求付费晚餐的位置，类似的结果。 做一次，可能没有人会知道或关心。 不过，继续大规模实施，我不知道为什么你会期望不受监管。

I mean, this is the case for a lot of things? Has always been the case.

If you host friends over for dinner at your house a lot, nobody will ever say you are subject to the same rules as a restaurant. You start letting other people host dinners at your house, and things could change. You start letting people solicit your place for paid dinners, similar outcome. Do it once, nobody will probably know or care. Continue to do it at scale, though, and I don't know why you would expect to not be subject to regulations.

在 HN 中查看
AnthonyMouse 2026/03/27 03:09
问题显然是政府不应该监管私人言论。他们通过这些规则时说“看看 Facebook 有多大，他们需要受到监管”，而实际问题是他们需要去中心化。但这些规则不仅仅适用于 Facebook，更糟糕的是，这些规则是在中心化服务的假设下设计的，因此它们巩固了应该消除的东西。
The problem is obviously that the government shouldn't be regulating private speech. They pass these rules by saying "look how big Facebook is, they need to be regulated" when the actual problem is that they need to be decentralized. But then the rules don't apply only to Facebook, and worse, are designed under the assumption of a centralized service so that they entrench the thing that should be eliminated.
在 HN 中查看
taeric 2026/03/27 04:06
但这没有什么明显的地方吗？ 其一，这种言论只能通过其他受监管的方式来完成。 举个简单的例子，你不能声称“言论自由”并建造一座远距离传输的无线电塔。 就这一点而言，你不能声称言论自由允许在你家里举办音乐会。 同样，您也不能主张言论自由来出租或出借您房屋的房间用于存储。

正如其他地方所指出的，如果您想努力连接和验证将与您的服务器通信的不同各方，那么您几乎肯定可以自由地这样做。

我是否认为这些负担可能会以一些令人担忧的方式强加给人们？ 当然。 但我们已经要求对个人可以在家里进行而无需检查的事情进行检查和其他类似活动。 参见食品工业。

But there is nothing obvious about this? For one, this is speech that can only be done using otherwise regulated means. You couldn't claim "free speech" and build a radio tower that transmits long distances, as an easy example. For that matter, you can't claim free speech to allow concerts at your house. You similarly could not claim free speech to rent or loan out rooms of your house for storage.

As has been pointed out elsewhere, if you want to take the effort to connect and verify the different parties that are going to communicate with your server, you are almost certainly going to remain free to do so.

Do I think there are probably some concerning ways those burdens can be placed on folks? Certainly. But we already require inspections and other similar activities for things that individuals can do at home without an inspection. See the food industry.

在 HN 中查看
renegat0x0 2026/03/27 02:55
我正在谈论很多事情。还有我的程序，还有数据。程序对我来说并不那么重要。它们只是完成工作的工具。

我所有的程序和数据都是开放的。这是任何人都可以拿起并随心所欲地使用的东西

- https://github.com/rumca-js/Internet-Places-Database - 我找到的域名

- https://github.com/rumca-js/Internet-feeds - 我找到的提要

- https://github.com/rumca-js/RSS-Link-Database-2026 - 2026 年的新闻

- https://github.com/rumca-js/RSS-Link-Database-2025 - 2025年的新闻等

有什么改变吗？我不知道。我运行网络爬虫。对我来说，查看我的爬虫从互联网上获取什么内容很有趣。它确实改变了我的生活，这些项目改变了我对互联网的看法。更加积极主动。

我认为有很多项目对利基群体有用。我想我在一个 repo 上有 390 颗星。我希望至少我的项目对他们有用。这是一个充满希望的想法。

I am talking about many things. Also about my programs, but also data. Programs are not that important for me. They are just vehicles to get the job done.

All my programs and data are open. It is something that anybody can pick up, and use as they wish

- https://github.com/rumca-js/Internet-Places-Database - domains I found

- https://github.com/rumca-js/Internet-feeds - feeds I found

- https://github.com/rumca-js/RSS-Link-Database-2026 - news from 2026

- https://github.com/rumca-js/RSS-Link-Database-2025 - news from 2025, etc.

Does make any change? I don't know. I run web crawlers. It is interesting for me to see what my crawlers pick up from the Internet. It did change my life, these project changed how I see the Internet. More pro-activly.

I think there are many projects which can be useful for niche groups. I suppose I have 390 stars on one repo. I hope at least my projects were useful for them. That is a hopeful thought.

在 HN 中查看
chromacity 2026/03/27 00:49
> 如果你只是具体谈论你的程序，其他人无法访问，我认为没有任何战斗？做你想做的事，没有人关心，甚至不会知道。

我可以在手机上进行吗？

> If you're only talking specifically about your program that no one else has access to, I don't think there is any battle? Do whatever you want, no one cares nor would even know about it.

Can I do it on my phone?

在 HN 中查看
ChadNauseam 2026/03/27 00:51
如果您购买谷歌 Pixel 9（谷歌发布设备树的最后一个版本），您可以在手机上做任何您想做的事情。我的 Pixel 运行的是我自己构建的 Android 版本
If you buy a google pixel 9 (the last version for which google released device trees), you can do anything you want on your phone. My pixel runs a version of android I built myself
在 HN 中查看
beeflet 2026/03/27 01:27
不，因为尽管电话与计算机由相同的部件制成，但实际上是完全不同的东西。

您不能只在手机上运行程序。你必须运行应用程序，这些应用程序需要得到政府和制造手机的公司的批准，这也会增加额外的费用。该手机还具有持续的蜂窝/GPS/wifi/bt-mesh 位置跟踪功能，并且用户永远无法在不损坏手机的情况下完全关闭它，因为甚至电池都粘在里面。

对于普通人来说，它基本上是完美的从属设备。每个人都需要一个来访问您的银行帐户、接收不安全的短信身份验证、与其他 NPC 交谈以及普遍参与新经济。

如果你不认为这是正确的，那么你实际上就会清空我那蠢蛋奶奶的银行账户，她不停地安装恶意软件，从各方面来说，2000 年代初的翻盖手机都更适合她。

No because a phone, despite being made from the same parts as a computer is actually a completely different thing.

You can't just run programs on your phone. You have to run apps, which require approved by the government and the company that made the phone, which tacks on additional fees as well. The phone also has constant cellular/GPS/wifi/bt-mesh location tracking, and it can never be completely turned off by the user without destroying the phone because even the batteries are glued in.

It's basicially the perfect slave device for your average goy. And everyone will need one to to access your bank account, recieve insecure SMS authentication, talk to other NPCs, and generally participate in the neo-economy.

If you don't think this is right, you are literally going to empty the bank account of my dumb ass grandma who can't stop installing malware, and in every way is better served by a flip phone from the early 2000's.

在 HN 中查看
AnthonyMouse 2026/03/27 03:14
> 如果你不认为这是正确的，那么你真的会清空我那蠢蛋奶奶的银行账户，她不停地安装恶意软件，从各方面来说，2000 年代初的翻盖手机都更适合她。

那你为什么这么要求 其他人的 移动电脑必须被锁定，而不是要求有人制造只能打电话的手机？

> If you don't think this is right, you are literally going to empty the bank account of my dumb ass grandma who can't stop installing malware, and in every way is better served by a flip phone from the early 2000's.

Then why are you demanding that everyone else's mobile computers have to be locked down instead of demanding that somebody make a mobile phone that only makes phone calls?

在 HN 中查看
beeflet 2026/03/27 00:23
如果任何人不喜欢你，他们都可以对你的自托管乳齿象实例提出法律投诉，这会让警察上门，就像奶昔把男孩带到院子里一样。
Anyone will be able to lob legal complaints against your self-hosted mastodon instance if they don't like you, which will bring cops to your door like milkshake brings boys to the yard.
在 HN 中查看
embedding-shape 2026/03/27 00:27
是的，再说一遍，您运行公共服务，期望必须遵守公共平台的法规，不确定为什么有人会期望其他东西。

我说的是在自托管场景中为自己创建/运行软件，不仅仅是“我运行该软件，但它是为了其他人”，而是真正的“我运行软件，它是为了我自己和/或我的家人，而不是其他人”。/

Yes, again, you run a public service, expect to have to follow regulations for public platforms, not sure why anyone would expect something else.

I was talking about creating/running software for yourself, in a self-hosted scenario, not just "I run the software, but it's for others" but really "I run software and it's for myself and/or my family, no one else"./

在 HN 中查看
beeflet 2026/03/27 00:49
社交网络、博客或其他任何东西的意义在于它是为他人服务的。此外，我认为人们有言论自由的权利，并且应该有能力在公共场所合理地发表言论（例如，通过博客、论坛或其他方式）。

我在之前的评论中所说的是，要求“年龄检查、加密后门和其他不良/烦人的东西”的法规也适用于小型主机，并且可能像 DMCA 一样被滥用（除非您在具有良好操作安全性的 tor/i2p 上托管）。

任何监管都是好的，因为它是针对一家“大而糟糕的上市公司”而制定的，这正是我不同意的核心观点。什么时候你会变成“大坏公司”？安娜的档案算数吗？他们接受捐款。这看起来不像是一个充实的世界观。

The point of a social network, or blogging or whatever is that it's for others. Furthermore, I think people have the right to free speech and should have the ability to reasonably address the public square (for example, with a blog, or a forum or something).

What I'm saying in the previous comment is that regulations requiring "Age checks, encryption backdoors and other bad/annoying stuff" also apply to small hosts and can be abused like DMCA (unless you are hosting on tor/i2p with good opsec).

It's this notion that any regulation is good because it's done on a "big bad public company" that is at the heart of what I disagree with. At what point do you become a "big bad company"? Does anna's archive count? they accept donations. It just doesn't seem like a fleshed-out worldview.

在 HN 中查看
embedding-shape 2026/03/27 00:57
> 我在之前的评论中所说的是，要求“年龄检查、加密后门和其他不良/烦人的东西”的法规也适用于小型主机，并且可能像 DMCA 一样被滥用（除非您在具有良好 opsec 的 tor/i2p 上托管）。

是的，就像即使只有您和您的面包店一样，您仍然必须遵守一系列健康和食品安全法规，因为您提供的东西可能会伤害人们。

我认为，对于互联网上向公众提供的平台和服务提出类似的要求并不是什么奇怪的事情。尽管我可能不会说这应该是直接非法的，但我不介意进行更多研究和了解如何在不侵犯人们私下行为的情况下防止最大的伤害。但是，自托管 Mastodon 实例是否连接到公共互联网以及公共或私人的其他实例？就我个人而言，我倾向于第一种。

> What I'm saying in the previous comment is that regulations requiring "Age checks, encryption backdoors and other bad/annoying stuff" also apply to small hosts and can be abused like DMCA (unless you are hosting on tor/i2p with good opsec).

Yes, just like even if it's just you and your bakery, you still have to follow a bunch of health and food safety regulations, as you're providing something people can be harmed by.

I don't think it's so out of this world to require similar things for platforms and services available to the public on the internet. Although I wouldn't maybe say it should be straight up illegal, I wouldn't mind more research and understanding of how we could prevent the biggest harms, without infringing on what people do in private. But then is a self-hosted Mastodon instance connected to the public internet and other instances in public or in private? Personally I'd lean towards the first.

在 HN 中查看
AnthonyMouse 2026/03/27 03:17
> 你什么时候会成为“大坏公司”？

收入超过美国GDP的0.1%或市场份额超过本国市场的10%。

> At what point do you become a "big bad company"?

Revenue exceeds 0.1% of US GDP or market share exceeds 10% of their own market.

在 HN 中查看
bullen 2026/03/27 01:16
我想说：

1）使用HTTP（安全不是去中心化的方式）。

2) Selfhost DNS 服务器（在实践中很难扩展）。

3) Selfhost SMTP 服务器（也很棘手）。

4) 了解并备份您的路由器（dd-wrt ​​或 iptables）。

JSON 超过 HTTP 就是这样。

XML 对于某些事情来说也不错；即使我了解虐待的后果。

I would say:

1) Use HTTP (secure is not the way to decentralize).

2) Selfhost DNS server (hard to scale in practice).

3) Selfhost SMTP server (also tricky).

4) Know and backup your router (dd-wrt or iptables).

JSON over HTTP is the way.

XML is not bad for certain things too; even if I understand the legacy of abuse.

在 HN 中查看
zrm 2026/03/27 01:45
> 使用 HTTP （安全不是去中心化的方式）。

这似乎不是有用的建议。如果您打算使用 HTTP ，那么不使用 Let's Encrypt 的实际优势基本上为零。

更好的选择是使用支持密钥分发替代方法（例如 QR 码、首次使用信任）的新协议，而不是不使用。

> Selfhost DNS 服务器（在实践中很难扩展）。

这实际上很容易做到。

> Use HTTP (secure is not the way to decentralize).

This doesn't seem like useful advice. If you're going to use HTTP at all there is essentially zero practical advantage in not using Let's Encrypt.

The better alternative would be to use new protocols that support alternative methods of key distribution (e.g. QR codes, trust on first use) instead of none.

> Selfhost DNS server (hard to scale in practice).

This is actually very easy to do.

在 HN 中查看
bullen 2026/03/27 02:03
Let's Encrypt 不属于我们这里的朋友。

DNS 对您自己来说很容易，但如果您为其他人（1000 多人）托管它并且它需要拥有世界上的所有域，那么它就会变得很困难。

Let's Encrypt is not part of our friends here.

DNS is easy for yourself, but if you host it for others (1000+ of people) and it needs to have all domains in the world, then it becomes a struggle.

在 HN 中查看
zrm 2026/03/27 02:57
Let's Encrypt 是一个击败了证书卡特尔的非营利组织。如果没有它，使用 HTTP 带来的主要问题是安全性很差。

DNS 每秒可以在 Raspberry Pi 上回答数千个查询，并在成本不到 500 美元的单个旧服务器硬件上回答疯狂的数字。

Let's Encrypt is a non-profit that defeated the certificate cartel. The main thing you get from using HTTP without it is bad security.

DNS can answer thousands of queries per second on a Raspberry Pi and crazy numbers on a single piece of old server hardware that costs less than $500.

在 HN 中查看
bullen 2026/03/27 03:53
没有根证书是去中心化的。

如果您的 DNS 端口被您的 ISP 关闭，您就不能让人们从外部使用您的 DNS 服务器，那么您就需要非去中心化的 Google 或 Amazon。

另外，要进行自托管，您不能只转发根 DNS 服务器所说的内容，您需要将所有域及其 IP 存储在一个巨大的数据库中。

No root certificate is decentralized.

If your DNS port is closed by your ISP, you can't have people use your DNS server from the outside and then you need Google or Amazon which are not decentralized.

Also to be selfhosted you can't just forward what root DNS servers say, you need to store all domains and their IPs in a huge database.

在 HN 中查看
almatia 2026/03/27 04:08
Matrix (JSON)-ActivityPub (XML) 有桥梁，Elixir 中有一个： https://github.com/technostructures/kazarma/
There are bridges for Matrix (JSON)-ActivityPub (XML), one in Elixir: https://github.com/technostructures/kazarma/
在 HN 中查看
pixl97 2026/03/27 01:21
1）那么如何验证客户端收到的http是您发送的http？
1) so how do you validate the http the client receives is the http you sent?
在 HN 中查看
forgotmypw17 2026/03/27 01:25
使用散列和 PKI 自行验证。是的，它需要引导，就像集中式 HTTPS 需要引导一样。
Validate it yourself with hashing and PKI. Yes, it needs bootstrapping, just like centralized HTTPS needs bootstrapping.
在 HN 中查看
bullen 2026/03/27 01:33
哇，谢谢！

另外，如果人们需要更多的食物来进行（分散的）思考：

https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2289

Wow, thanks!

Also if people need more food for (decentralized) thought:

https://datatracker.ietf.org/doc/html/rfc2289

在 HN 中查看
hermitShell 2026/03/27 00:15
您如何看待硬件级别自由的步伐？我的上下文也来自科里·多克托罗： https://youtu.be/3C1Gnxhfok0?si=RjmADE5pQ3s7fBIk

对我来说，拥有自己的计算机的自由意味着我可以在上面运行任何我想要的软件。

自托管通常以计算的某种开放性为基础。有趣的是，它实际上仍然不允许您使用某些服务，例如谷歌地图，即使最终用户有很大的好处，他们也可以免费获得它，因为他们回馈了他们的数据。

What do you think of the pace of hardware level freedoms? My context is also from Corey Doctorow: https://youtu.be/3C1Gnxhfok0?si=RjmADE5pQ3s7fBIk

For me the freedom to own my computer means I can run any software I want on it.

Self hosting is predicated on some openness of computing in general. Interestingly it still does not practically allow you to use certain services like Google Maps, where even if the end user has great benefit, they get it for free because they give back their data.

在 HN 中查看
beeflet 2026/03/27 00:21
有 openstreetmap 地图应用程序（OSMand、有机地图）
There are the openstreetmap mapping apps (OSMand, organic maps)
在 HN 中查看
fsflover 2026/03/27 00:26
应该使用有机地图而不是 OSMand， https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=42343121

或者也许是 Comaps， https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=43961908

Organic maps should be used instead of OSMand, https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=42343121

Or maybe Comaps, https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=43961908

在 HN 中查看
handedness 2026/03/27 01:13
我和你一样喜欢有机地图而不是 OsmAnd，虽然我日常驾驶 CoMap 的时间不长（实际上也没有任何人），但我已经非常喜欢它而不是有机地图。我需要使用它足够长的时间才能看到边缘情况是什么样的，但是在使用了三个时区的农村地区和人口稠密的城市之后，它效果很好。
I share your preference for Organic Maps over OsmAnd, and while I haven't been daily-driving CoMaps for long (nor has anyone, really) I already significantly prefer it over Organic Maps. I need to use it long enough to see what the edge cases are like, but after using it three time zones worth of rural places and dense cities, it has worked well.

在 HN 中查看
fsflover 2026/03/27 00:13
> 我如何赢得这场与大科技的战斗？

支持 https://edri.org 和 https://noyb.eu

> How do I win this battle with big tech?

Support https://edri.org and https://noyb.eu

在 HN 中查看
sixothree 2026/03/26 23:29
如果你看看医疗保健领域，你会意识到互操作性的存在只是因为政府计划强制要求患者拥有自己的数据，并且必须能够及时访问所有这些数据；还定义了该数据的格式（开源定义）。

您还可以以某种有意义的方式定义“存在”。您还可以意识到付款人正在侵入互操作性数据交换的各个方面。

If you look at the healthcare space, you will realize interoperability only exists because it was mandated by government programs that the patient owns their data and must be provided timely access to all of that data; and also defines specifies the format of that data (open source definitions).

You might also define "exists" in some sort of way that makes sense. And you can also realize that payers are encroaching on every aspect of interoperability data exchange.

在 HN 中查看
skywal_l 2026/03/26 23:44
之所以强制这样做，是因为在某些情况下，从患者那里获取数据实际上是有害的。 CT 扫描不是良性的。因此，为了确保制造商 A 的 CT 扫描可以在制造商 B 的审查站上读取，DICOM 标准应运而生。

但它确实有健康因素。尽管我完全同意标准对消费者有利，但这里的激励措施并不那么强大。

It was mandated because, in some cases, getting data from the patient is actually harmful. A CT scan is not benign. So to ensure that CT scans from manufacturer A could be read on a review station of manufacturer B, the DICOM standard was created.

But there is a real health element to it. Although I perfectly agree that standards are good for the consumer, the incentives here are not as strong.

在 HN 中查看
taeric 2026/03/27 00:08
同样，对于不能向谁提供数据也有很多控制措施。 健康记录并不是对所有人免费开放的。
There are also similarly a lot of controls mandated on who they cannot give the data to. It isn't like health records are an open free for all.
在 HN 中查看
jabl 2026/03/27 00:40
我对医疗保健 IT 项目管理一无所知，但就在前几天，当地新闻中提到，该地区（约有 100 万居民）花费数年时间和大约 8 亿欧元投入生产的全能型医疗保健应用程序反响非常差，以至于他们正在考虑从头开始。看到我的税钱花得值，我很高兴...

这是一种称为 MUMPS 的实现，这显然是美国的一些系统，非常神秘但广泛使用。

再说一次，我不是这个主题的专家，但它确实看起来像是标准、API、文件格式等等将是系统的关键，其中解耦的组件可以随着时间的推移逐步发展，而不是当前的系统，后者似乎是一个巨大的整体。

I know nothing about IT project management for healthcare, but just the other day over here in the local news there was a mention that the all-singing-all-dancing healthcare application that the region (with ~1M inhabitants) has been spending years and around 800 million euros to get into production has been so poorly received that they're considering starting over from scratch. I'm so happy seeing my tax money well spent...

This is an implementation of something called MUMPS, which is apparently some US system that is very arcane but widely used.

Again, I'm not an expert on this topic, but it indeed seems like standards, API's, file formats and whatnot would be keys to a system where decoupled components can be evolved step-by-step over time instead of the current system which seems to be a humongous monolith.

在 HN 中查看
sixothree 2026/03/27 05:15
考虑到美国对 FHIR 的参与程度，这对我来说听起来很疯狂。

http://hl7.org/fhir/

即使您不关心这些东西，FHIR 也绝对值得研究。

Which sounds crazy to me considering how much involvement the US has with FHIR.

http://hl7.org/fhir/

Even if you don't care about this stuff, FHIR is definitely worth investigating.

在 HN 中查看
strangattractor 2026/03/27 01:02
互操作性使网络成为可能。我不确定它是否会在人们行为没有发生根本改变的情况下拯救它。

想象一下，您是一个 20 世纪 60 年代的家庭，RCA 试图向您出售一台只能观看 ABC 的电视，而 Zenith 则拥有一台只能观看 CBS 的电视。在我看来，60 年后，普通人将无法观看线性电视。 “让广告为此买单”让整个行业变得更加糟糕的情况以前也没有发生过。

Interoperability is what made the Web possible. Not sure I buy that it will save it without a fundamental change in people's behavior.

Image you are a 1960s household and RCA tries to sell you a TV to only watch ABC and Zenith has a TV to only watch CBS. 60 years later linear TV is unwatchable by normal humans IMO. It's not like "let advertising pay for this" enshittifying an entire industry hasn't happened before.

在 HN 中查看

amelius 2026/03/27 00:26

At some point this will be solved by AI, where you can just say: hey chatgpt, turn this powerpoint file into a keynote file.
在 HN 中查看
goldenarm 2026/03/27 00:55
人工智能正在快速毒害数据采集和自动化。 它产生了如此多的垃圾邮件，现在每个人都开始阻止机器人。
AI is poisoning the well of scraping and automation really fast. It generates so much spam, everyone is starting to block robots now.
在 HN 中查看
wslh 2026/03/27 05:24 deleted
[死的]

在 HN 中查看
nh23423fefe 2026/03/26 23:20
> 我们可以为 Twitter 提出两种不同的规则……一个端到端的原则……如果我关注某人，并且他们发布了一些内容，我就可以看到它。这条规则使得 Twitter 很难过多地关注其首选供应商的内容。最重要的是，我们添加了退出权。这是在不失去关注者和被关注者的情况下离开 Twitter 的权利。这将是建立 API 的授权，

我什至不明白第一点是什么，甚至提议，现在立法用例？这肯定是推特推崇“法西斯主义”的狗口哨，完全取决于“超重”这个狡猾的词

第二个陈述似乎是一个类别错误。您可以通过什么方式离开 Twitter，但仍然保留关注者和被关注者。这些词只在 Twitter 的上下文中有意义。我们在这个世界上没有任何关系。如果我在 Twitter 上关注某人，然后他们行使“退出和保留权”，我现在是否会在 Tiktok、mastodon 和 telegram 等上关注他们？不，当然不是。

假设我讨厌纳粹并在推特上关注所有纳粹。现在我行使退出权。 Twitter 会被迫向我提供哪些有关我讨厌的人的数据？

> we can propose two different rules for Twitter ... an end-to-end principle ... If I follow someone, and they post something, I can see it. That rule makes it really hard for Twitter to overweight content from its preferred suppliers. On top of that we add the Right to Exit. This is the right to leave Twitter without losing your followers and followees. This would be a mandate to stand up an API,

I don't even understand what the first point is even proposing, legislating use cases now? It's gotta be some dog whistle about Twitter pushing "fascism" and entirely hinges on the weasel word "overweight"

The second statement just seems like a category error. In what way can you leave twitter yet still retain followers and followees. Those words only mean something in the context of Twitter. We have no relationship in the world. If I follow someone on twitter and then they exercise their "right to exit and retain" do i now follow them on tiktok and mastodon and telegram and etc. No of course not.

Suppose I hate nazis and follow all the nazis on twitter. Now I exercise my right to exit. What data about the people I hate will Twitter be forced to provide me?

在 HN 中查看
MSFT_Edging 2026/03/26 23:55
这是一个可观察的指标。如果我不关注任何“回归传统”Statue PFP 风格的海报，与我关注的人、他们关注的人以及他们与谁互动的帖子相比，“为你”选项卡应该多久向您显示这些风格的帖子？

收购前后这是一个明显的转变。只有当我关注的人有目的地与他们互动时，我才会看到这种风格的海报。收购后，我的动态中开始出现更多反移民、支持白人、支持民族主义风格的帖子。

It's an observable metric. If I don't follow any "return to tradition" Statue PFP style posters, how often should the "for you" tab show you those style posts compared to posts by people I follow, who they follow, and who they interact with?

Pre and post acquisition it was a clear shift. I would only see that style of poster when people I followed purposefully interacted with them. Post Acquisition, I began to get many more anti-immigrant, pro-white, pro-nationalism style posts in my feed.

在 HN 中查看
lokar 2026/03/26 23:24
对我来说，超重的使用似乎很清楚。 您有一个按某些分数排序的项目提要，在计算分数时，您必须权衡不同的因素。

看起来很清楚，主题中立

The use of overweight seems clear to me. You have a feed of items sorted by some score, when calculating scores you have to weight different factors.

Seems pretty clear, and subject neutral

在 HN 中查看
